title: How to make a mordant colour-sample record
subtitle: Building a permanent reference card that shows one dye plant's colour on wool across four mordants
excerpt: A mordant sample record is a physical card or notebook page with small samples of dyed wool stapled or sewn in a grid: the same dye plant applied to wool pre-mordanted with alum, iron, copper, and a tannin-only control. Each sample is labelled with the dye plant, mordant, percentage WOF, dye-bath temperature, and hold time. The record builds into a reference library for planning future dye projects.
sourceNotes: Mordant sample-record keeping is consistent practice in UK natural dye guilds and documented in Jenny Dean's 'Wild Colour'. Grid format and labelling conventions are synthesised from natural dye studio practice.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral or plant compound used to fix natural dye to fibre. Different mordants produce different colours from the same dye plant: alum typically gives clear, warm tones; iron shifts colours toward darker, greener tones; copper shifts toward blue-green. A mordant sample record documents these differences from a single dye bath.

The dry weight of the fibre before dyeing, used as the reference unit for calculating mordant and dye-plant quantities. Labelling sample records with WOF percentages makes them reproducible: knowing that 15% WOF alum was used means anyone can replicate the result with any quantity of fibre.

An iron sulphate solution used either as a pre-mordant or as an after-bath to shift and darken plant-dye colours. On most flavonoid dyes, iron shifts warm yellows and tans toward muted greens and khaki. Iron is one of the four standard mordant columns in a mordant sample record.

A group of astringent plant compounds that have some affinity for protein and cellulose fibres without a metallic mordant. A tannin-only control in a mordant sample record shows the colour the dye plant produces on un-mordanted fibre, which is usually lighter and less fast than a mordanted result.

content.text: A mordant colour-sample record is a practical tool: a card or notebook page that shows exactly what colour one dye plant produces on wool across different
mordants
. The grid has one row per dye plant and one column per mordant. Over time it becomes a reference library for colour planning. A well-labelled record includes the dye plant, mordant type,
weight of fibre
(WOF) percentage, dye-bath temperature, hold time, and the date. The date matters:
tannin
-rich dyes fade with light. Comparing a fresh sample with a year-old one shows true lightfastness. This reading covers the card format, sample bundles, labelling, and reading the four-mordant grid.

attrs.title: Mordant safety
attrs.body: Work outdoors or with an extractor fan. Dedicated pots only. Gloves, apron, closed-toe shoes. Keep children and pets out of the dye area.

content.text: The four-mordant grid
The standard sample record uses four columns for each dye plant:
Alum mordant (alum potash at 15% WOF): the baseline mordant that gives clear, warm tones. Most natural dye books use alum as the reference mordant.
Iron modifier (iron sulphate at 2 to 4% WOF, used as an after-bath): darkens and greens the alum result. Labelled as 'alum + iron' on the card since the iron follows the alum pre-mordant.

Copper mordant (copper sulphate at 2% WOF): shifts colours toward blue-green and teal. Copper is the most hazardous of the common mordants; handle with full PPE and dispose of the spent bath down a foul drain only.

No mordant (tannin-only control): the colour the dye plant produces on un-mordanted wool. Usually lighter and less saturated than any of the mordanted results. Useful as a baseline for understanding how much the mordant contributes to the final colour.

Pour spent iron and copper mordant baths down a foul drain only. Never pour into a storm drain, a stream, a garden soak-away, or any watercourse. Iron and copper sulphate in accumulated quantities are harmful to aquatic life.
Making the sample bundles

Small wrapped fibre bundles are the most practical sample format. Each bundle is a length of wool yarn (about 1 gram of un-spun roving or 2 metres of yarn) wound around a small piece of card, secured at both ends, and labelled before dyeing. The card insert keeps the bundle flat and provides a surface for labelling after dyeing. Write the mordant type on the card insert in pencil before the bundle enters the dye bath; ink runs.

Four bundles per dye plant, one per mordant column, are prepared and dyed simultaneously from the same dye bath to ensure the only variable between columns is the mordant. A separate mordant bath is prepared for each mordant type before the bundles enter the shared dye bath.
The record card format

A piece of thick watercolour card (A5 or A4) works well as the base. Staple or sew each bundle to the card in the grid position. Use stainless steel staples rather than plated ones, which rust over time. Alternatively, sew each bundle through the card with a few stitches of linen thread.
Label each sample position in pencil with:
Dye plant: common name and Latin name (e.g. weld, Reseda luteola)
Mordant type and percentage WOF (e.g. alum 15% WOF)
Dye plant quantity as percentage WOF (e.g. 100% WOF dried weld)
Dye bath temperature in °C and hold time in minutes
Date dyed
Water source (tap, filtered, or rain): useful in areas with high iron content in the water supply
Archival storage

Store the completed cards in a folder or box away from direct light. Exposure to sunlight is the main cause of fading in the record; keeping the folder closed between uses means the record remains accurate over years. A second set of duplicate cards stored in full sunlight on a windowsill is a practical lightfastness test: comparing the two sets after six months shows exactly how much each dye fades in real conditions.
How to read the results
Scan across a single row (one dye plant, four mordants) to see the range of colours available from a single plant. The
iron modifier

column typically shows the darkest and most shifted result. Scan down a single column (one mordant, multiple dye plants) to see which dye plants give similar tones with that mordant, which is useful for colour-matching or substituting a plant that is out of season.

Ten to fifteen dye plants across four mordants is enough to plan a complete natural dye project from one reference card set. Good starting plants: weld, onion skins (yellow and red), madder, chamomile, blackberry leaves, oak bark, and meadowsweet. Each gives a distinct response across the four mordant columns. Together they cover yellow through rust to green-grey.
